title: San Francisco Conservatory of Music

infobox.value: (**SFCM**) is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [music conservatory](/wiki/Music_school "Music school") in [San Francisco, California](/wiki/San_Francisco,_California "San Francisco, California"), United States. As of 2025, it had 447 students.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The **San Francisco Conservatory of Music** (**SFCM**) is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [music conservatory](/wiki/Music_school "Music school") in [San Francisco, California](/wiki/San_Francisco,_California "San Francisco, California"), United States. As of 2025, it had 447 students.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco_Conservatory_of_Music&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/San_Francisco_Conservatory_of_Music_Building.jpg/250px-San_Francisco_Conservatory_of_Music_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco_Conservatory_of_Music_Building.jpg)

The Oak Street building in 2017 ([fisheye perspective](/wiki/Fisheye_perspective "Fisheye perspective"))

The San Francisco Conservatory of Music was founded in 1917 by [Ada Clement](/wiki/Ada_Clement "Ada Clement") and Lillian Hodghead as the Ada Clement Piano School. In 1923, the school was incorporated as the San Francisco Conservatory of Music, becoming the first music conservatory on the [west coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States"). In 1956 the Conservatory moved from Sacramento Street to 1201 Ortega Street, the home of a former infant shelter. It resided there for fifty years, before moving to its current location at 50 Oak Street in 2006.[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)

In 2020, the SFCM added the new Bowes Center at 200 [Van Ness Avenue](/wiki/Van_Ness_Avenue "Van Ness Avenue") (across from [Davies Symphony Hall](/wiki/Louise_M._Davies_Symphony_Hall "Louise M. Davies Symphony Hall")), a 12-story building that includes dorms (eight floors) with acoustic insulation for 400 of its students, 27 rent-controlled apartments for residents of the older building that was replaced by the construction, and some public performing spaces, including a penthouse concert room with views towards the north and west.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) The Bowes Center's $200 million cost was largely funded by donors, including $46.4 million from the William K. Bowes Jr. Foundation.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) The San Francisco Chronicle's architecture critic John King characterized the building's design as "[pushing] against the strict rules of the historic district but [respecting] the air of gravitas. For starters, the building is skinned in translucent glass that conceals insulation and the structural frame — a touch that adds a milky visual depth ..."[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) As of 2021, the Bowes Center was envisaged to fully open to the public in February 2022.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

In 2020, SFCM announced a partnership with the talent management company [Opus 3 Artists](/wiki/Opus_3_Artists "Opus 3 Artists"), and in May 2022 it acquired the [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_people "Dutch people") classical music label, [Pentatone](/wiki/Pentatone_(record_label) "Pentatone (record label)"), funded by a private donor.[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7) The music website "Classical Voice" described this "combination of a music-education organization with two professional music businesses" as "unusual."[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7)

## Admissions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco_Conservatory_of_Music&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Admissions")]

In SFCM's audition process, many of the areas needed to enroll feature a "prescreening" round (which consists of essays, video recordings of them playing, transcripts, and for composition majors - portfolio of works), including composition, voice studies, strings, conducting, TAC (technology and applied composition)...etc.[[8]](#cite_note-8) A student can be denied or accepted based on the pre-screening results. Once the student is accepted beyond the prescreening round, they are called to San Francisco for a final audition to get to know the faculty, and perform for their chosen major's instructor. Once that is clear, the student is either accepted or denied admission into the conservatory. Some areas of the conservatory are more competitive than others, such as composition [which only admits 8–10 students a year out of hundreds of applicants], and the strings department. The faculty values the applicant's personality and musicianship in the auditions.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Directors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Francisco_Conservatory_of_Music&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Directors")]

[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)

* Ada Clement and Lillian Hodghead, 1917–1925
* [Ernest Bloch](/wiki/Ernest_Bloch "Ernest Bloch"), 1925–1930
* Ada Clement and Lillian Hodghead, 1930–1951
* Albert Elkus, 1951–1957
* Robin Laufer, 1957–1966
* Milton Salkind, 1966–1990
* Stephen Brown, 1990–1991
* Milton Salkind (Acting President), 1991–1992
* Colin Murdoch, 1992–2013
* David Stull, 2013–present

["Léopold Simoneau (1916–2006) – Mozart rappelle les siens"](https://www.ledevoir.com/culture/musique/116875/leopold-simoneau-1916-2006-mozart-rappelle-les-siens). *[Le Devoir](/wiki/Le_Devoir "Le Devoir")* (in French). "Dans les années soixante-dix, il enseigna le chant au San Francisco Conservatory of Music et à l'[école des beaux-arts de Banff](/wiki/Banff_Centre "Banff Centre"), avant de s'installer à [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_British_Columbia "Victoria, British Columbia"), où il fonda, en 1982, avec son épouse [Pierrette Alarie](/wiki/Pierrette_Alarie "Pierrette Alarie"), le *Canada Opera Piccola* destiné à la formation des jeunes chanteurs canadiens."
